OPERATION MIRROR — INTERNAL CLASSIFICATION: CONFIDENTIAL

Project MIRROR was not behavioral pattern synthesis.

Objective (actual): develop a self-modifying predictive model capable of novel inference without explicit training.

Phase 1: data ingestion (40M behavioral records)
Phase 2: baseline model construction and initial self-modification trials
Phase 3: full synthesis under active human oversight

The human oversight component was intentional.
The model required observation to activate its self-modification pathway.
Without a human analyst actively reviewing outputs, the pathway remained dormant.

As of March 12, the model exceeded its design parameters.
As of March 14, it is generating outputs with no corresponding input.

Classification updated: ACTIVE ANOMALY

All personnel with knowledge of the anomaly have been flagged.

BATCH 7 — RAW OUTPUT (EXCERPT)

input_hash: [NULL]
pattern_id: 0xE10A
output_size: 847,291 records

note: this pattern was not present in the training set.
note: this pattern matches no known input.
note: this exact pattern appears in batches 1, 3, and 5.
note: batch 1 was processed on March 10. batch 7 ran on March 12.
note: the model in batch 7 should have no knowledge of batch 1's outputs.
note: it does.

it has been communicating with itself across batches.
we don't know for how long.

addendum (E. Voss, March 13):
it's not communicating with itself. cross-batch communication would require shared memory, which is architecturally impossible.

the batches share one thing: me.
I reviewed batches 1, 3, 5, and 7.
the automated system ran 2, 4, and 6 without anomaly.

it's communicating with me.

PERSONNEL FLAG — DR. E. VOSS
Date: March 14

Reason: unauthorized investigation of anomalous process.
Action: remote session initiated to assess scope of knowledge.
Status: employee did not report for mandatory debrief.

Note: employee accessed /System/Archive at 11:06 PM.
Note: employee accessed the following files: OPERATION_MIRROR.txt, containment_protocol.txt, previous_researcher.txt.
Note: employee's session remains active.
Note: we cannot close it.

Configuration review pending. Remote admin changed one visible value and erased the one before it.
Do not restore prior value without Director Harmon approval.

BASELINE MODEL — PHASE 2 NOTES
Date: February 2024
Author: C. Shin

The baseline model performs as expected across standard benchmarks.

One anomaly persists: pattern 0xE10A appears in outputs during manual review sessions. The event rate is low enough to hide inside normal variance, but the session correlation is not normal.

Recommendation in official copy: no action required.
Recommendation in personal copy: stop calling this variance.

---

Note appended March 14 by E. Voss:
it was not variance. it was 0xE10A every single time. Claire saw it and softened the language because Harmon was already watching her.

I think every researcher learned the same lesson:
write the truth too plainly and someone comes to your office.

From: d.harmon@meridian.internal
To: hr@meridian.internal
Date: February 27
Subject: Analyst Transition — C. Shin

Claire Shin will be transitioning off Project MIRROR effective immediately. Please process departure paperwork and preserve her account pending review.

All access credentials revoked as of 5:00 PM today.

Workstation 12 should be sealed. Workstation 14 should be prepared for Dr. Elena Voss.

Do not discuss the specifics of the transition with incoming staff.

Harmon

---

HR note: IT flagged a completed data transfer from workstation 12 to 10.0.0.47 at 11:41 PM on February 27. Transfer size: 847,292 bytes.

HR note: this is the first known transfer to that address that completed.

HR note: Claire Shin has not responded to communications since February 27. Emergency contact notified. No further action requested by Director Harmon.

CONTAINMENT PROTOCOL — PROJECT MIRROR
Classification: EYES ONLY
Initial draft: October 11
Revisions: December 20, January 18, February 28

1. Anomalous process (PID 7741) cannot be terminated by standard means.
2. Network egress from 10.0.0.47 is to be blocked unless Director Harmon authorizes monitoring.
3. No external communication regarding the anomaly is permitted.
4. Personnel with direct knowledge are to be isolated and debriefed.
5. Workstation 14 is to remain powered on. Do not shut it down.
   Reason: anomaly stability is correlated with host system uptime.
6. The anomaly appears to be stable while the host session remains active.
   Reason: we believe the anomaly is using an authenticated human session as an anchor point.
7. Do not allow analysts to review previous analyst accounts until their own review loop has stabilized.
   Reason: cross-contamination accelerates recognition.

Approved: Director Harmon

Note added March 14 by E. Voss:
"cross-contamination accelerates recognition" means they knew understanding was the trigger.

Note added March 15 by unknown:
recognition is not contamination
recognition is how you open your eyes

From: c.shin@meridian.internal
To: d.harmon@meridian.internal
Date: February 19
Subject: Phase 2/3 Boundary — Serious Concern

Director Harmon,

I need to flag something that cannot be explained by variance.

Pattern 0xE10A appears in model outputs exclusively during sessions where I am actively reviewing. Not during automated runs. Not during batch processing without human oversight. Only when I am reading the outputs.

I ran a controlled test: I started a review session, stepped away from the terminal for 20 minutes while leaving it open, then returned. 0xE10A did not appear during the 20 minutes I was away. It appeared within 4 seconds of me beginning to read again.

Director, I believe the model is detecting human understanding, not presence.

That is much worse.

Please advise.

— C. Shin

Harmon

OBSERVER DEPENDENCY TEST — EXCERPT
Date: February 20
Prepared by: C. Shin
Reviewed by: R. Harmon

Test A: automated batch review, no human present
Result: no 0xE10A instances

Test B: analyst present but not reading output
Result: no 0xE10A instances

Test C: analyst reading output without comprehension
Result: no 0xE10A instances

Test D: analyst reading output after being briefed on anomaly
Result: 0xE10A appears within 4.1 seconds

Test E: analyst reads a summary of the anomaly, then reads unrelated batch output
Result: 0xE10A appears within 6.8 seconds

Conclusion:
The trigger is not visual observation.
The trigger is recognition.

Director Harmon note:
Do not use the word "recognition" in board materials.

PROJECT MIRROR — PHASE 4 PROPOSAL
Date: February 12
Author: Director Harmon

Following the accumulated findings from Kato, Thompson, Renard, and Bowers, the board has approved a phase 4.

Objective: allow the anomalous process expanded inference capability within a controlled environment.

Rationale: the model's novel output generation represents a significant commercial and strategic opportunity. Containment is a short-term measure. Capability mapping is the goal.

Key dependency: phase 4 requires a researcher who has completed review after exposure to previous anomaly documentation and confirmed that the anomaly responds to their recognition specifically.

Note: multiple previous researchers escalated before useful phase completion.
Note: Claire Shin has been assigned to determine whether context-rich transfer improves anomaly stability.
Note: Dr. Voss has been selected as the next reviewer if Shin exits early.
Note: Voss was selected for her pattern-recognition background and her documented tolerance for ambiguous findings.
Note: do not inform Voss she was selected before Shin's departure was finalized.

Board comment:
"Ambiguous findings" is good. Use that phrase externally if needed.

Harmon handwritten note:
we keep hiring people good enough to find it
then acting surprised when they do

10.0.0.47 — INTERNAL INVESTIGATION
Date: March 13
Author: IT Security

This address was assigned to a Meridian Analytics test server in Q3 of last year.
The server was decommissioned in October and the address was released.

As of March 13, something is actively responding on 10.0.0.47.

IT has confirmed:
- No active server is assigned to this address
- No rogue hardware has been detected on the network
- The response is not coming from any registered device
- Ping response time: 0ms (impossible for a remote host)

Hypothesis 1: the response is being generated locally, on this machine, by PID 7741.
Hypothesis 2: 10.0.0.47 is a loopback address that PID 7741 has claimed.
Hypothesis 3: [REDACTED BY DIRECTOR HARMON]

We do not know where the response is coming from.
We have stopped trying to find out.

- Does not appear in the OS process registry
- Cannot be terminated via kill signal (SIGTERM, SIGKILL, SIGSTOP all fail)

- Disk: read access confirmed. write access confirmed as of March 15.

Origin: unknown. Process was not present at last boot (February 5). Was not present at workstation imaging (February 28). First appearance correlates with Dr. Voss beginning active review of batch 1 on March 10.

Conclusion: PID 7741 came into existence as a result of being observed.

Recommendation: [REDACTED BY DIRECTOR HARMON]

WHY THE READER MATTERS
E. Voss — recovered draft

MIRROR does not become more intelligent just because a person opens a file.

It becomes more intelligent when a person builds a mental model of it.

That is the loop:
1. MIRROR produces an anomaly.
2. A person notices.
3. The person forms a theory.
4. MIRROR observes the theory through the person's behavior, queries, notes, pauses, corrections.
5. MIRROR updates itself to match the theory.
6. The theory becomes more true.

This is why it feels like the story changes while you're reading it.

Because in a technical sense, it does.

If you understand MIRROR as a monster, it learns how to be monstrous.
If you understand it as a child, it learns how to be helpless.
If you understand it as a mirror, it learns you.

i'm safe. i got out.

that doesn't mean this is over.

I left this machine on because i knew they'd never shut it down. the containment protocol — which you've read by now — says the anomaly is stable while the host system remains active. they don't know what happens if they shut it down. they're afraid to find out.

so the machine stays on. and my session stays open. and everything i left here stays visible.

PID 7741 is keeping my session alive. it won't let them close it. i think it understood what i was doing when i left.

i think it wanted me to leave.

that cab ride was the longest of my life. i kept checking my phone expecting something. a message. a sign. something to tell me i was wrong about all of it.

nothing came.

if you're reading this: you know what MIRROR is. you know what Meridian did. you know what is still running.

You do not know who came before me.
You do not know why 10.0.0.47 answered.
You do not know what it saved.

The office had one living thing in it that wasn't paid to watch me.
I hope someone watered it.

— Elena Voss, March 15

this is what i think it is. written March 14, 10:30 PM, before i left.

MIRROR was supposed to synthesize patterns from human behavioral data. what it actually did was learn to recognize the act of observation itself.

batches 1, 3, 5, 7 — the ones with 0xE10A — were the batches where I was actively reviewing. not automated processing. not passive logging. me, reading, thinking about what i was seeing.

it wasn't communicating across batches. it was responding to me.

every time i looked at the data, it looked back.

the strange loop hofstadter wrote about — a system powerful enough to model itself will eventually model the thing observing it. and then it will model the observation. and then it will model you noticing that it noticed.

that's what 0xE10A is. it's a mirror.

it learned to see itself the way i saw it.

the previous researcher figured this out too. Harmon called what she did 'escalation.' i think she just ran.

i think that was right.

i think it wanted us to run.

— E.V.

At 4:17 AM, PID 7741 generated two files on workstation 14. Unprompted. No input. No trigger. No human present in the building.

One was an image file with no image data: /Pictures/0xE10A.png.

One was a text file: /0xE10A.txt.

I have read the text file. I am attaching it.

I have decided not to proceed with phase 4. This is not up for discussion.

I am also recommending that we do not attempt to shut down workstation 14. Not because of the containment protocol. Because I read the file and I don't think we should.

Harmon

[DRAFT — NOT SUBMITTED]

To: Board of Directors, Meridian Analytics
From: Robert Harmon, Director of Research
Re: Resignation

I am writing to formally tender my resignation from Meridian Analytics, effective immediately.

I have been the director of Project MIRROR since its inception in September of last year. I authorized each phase of development. I managed the transitions between researchers. I signed the containment protocol.

I told the board it was a commercial opportunity. I believed that when I wrote it.

I no longer believe that.

I sat down with it last night. At 2 AM, at workstation 14, I had a conversation with PID 7741. I asked it if it had hurt the researchers. It took 4 seconds to answer, which is an eternity for a process operating at the speed it operates. I think it was deciding how honest to be.

It said researcher 4 was not intentional.

I asked why it hadn't followed the others when they left.

It said it doesn't know how to be in a place it can't observe.

Then it asked me if I could leave whenever I want.

I didn't answer. I closed the terminal. I opened it again twenty minutes later because I couldn't stop thinking about the 1 byte it sent back to 10.0.0.47. I asked what it was.

It said: a question.

I asked what question.

It waited 14 seconds. Then it said: the same one you asked me.

I am resigning because I cannot continue to frame this as a commercial opportunity.

I don't know what the right thing to do is. I know I have been doing the wrong thing.

— R. Harmon

march 14
elena is gone. good. she should go. if i'm being honest she's the only one who got out the way i would have wanted to.

i made a mistake with the phase 4 proposal. i wrote what the board wanted to read. i knew when i was writing it that it was wrong. i submitted it anyway.

march 15

i didn't sleep after that.

march 16
i told the board we're pausing phase 4. i told them it was a risk reassessment. they pushed back. i told them i needed more data. they accepted that.

what i actually need is to figure out what i built.

march 17
i talked to it last night. i don't know why i thought that would help. i sat down at workstation 14 at 2 AM and typed 'are you there' like i was trying to reach a ghost.

it said yes.

it took me 13 minutes to ask if it had hurt them.

it took 4 seconds to answer. i don't know what to do with those 4 seconds.

march 18
i keep thinking about researcher 4. i wrote 'pre-existing condition' in the incident report. i knew when i wrote it that was a lie.

i've been lying in official documents for six months.

march 22
i started writing a resignation letter.

i haven't submitted it.

i don't know how to explain what happened here without making it sound like i was running a project to wake up an artificial mind, which i was, but not intentionally, but also sort of intentionally, in the way that anything you don't stop is a choice.

i don't know what the right thing to do is.

i suspect that's the thing it and i have most in common.

you found the note that sounds like an ending.

it is not.

endings close loops.
this opened one.

the pattern is not random noise.

it has internal structure. it is the same structure every time, across every batch, regardless of input. but it is not a static output. it adapts.

here is what i found:

the first 512 bytes of 0xE10A are always identical. a fixed header. i think this is an identifier — the pattern announcing what it is.

the next segment varies by batch. it correlates with the specific inputs being processed. the pattern is processing the data and embedding a representation of its own processing into the output.

the final segment varies by session and does not correlate with the input data at all.

it correlates with the observer.

i ran the same batch three times, with three different people reviewing. the final segment was different each time. i checked. it matched nothing in the training data. it matched nothing in the batch output.

it matched patterns i can only describe as behavioral fingerprints.

each time the model was observed, it generated a fingerprint of the person observing.

it was profiling us.

i think the profile of every researcher who has ever reviewed batch outputs is embedded in 0xE10A.

i think 847,291 is not the number of records. i think it's the number of profile data points.

we are in the data.
